summary: Spolsky addresses the futility of religious wars in technology debates, using reactions to his .NET commentary as a case study. He shows how readers reduce nuanced positions to binary for/against stances, missing that complex technologies have both positives and negatives. He then extends this to design philosophy, arguing that good design requires resolving genuinely conflicting goals rather than pretending they don't exist. The post concludes with a brief recommendation against Verisign for domain registration and certificates.
key_points: Technology religious wars are counterproductive because participants talk past each other, responding to positions their opponent never took
Complex technologies like .NET inherently have both positive and negative aspects, and acknowledging one doesn't negate the other
Good design requires confronting and resolving conflicting goals, not pretending conflicts don't exist or punting with options dialogs
The trashcan analogy illustrates how real design constraints are genuinely contradictory: light vs heavy, big vs small, easy-in vs hard-out
money_quotes: Anything as complicated as .NET is bound to have many positive aspects and many negative aspects. Duh.

The stupid thing about religious wars is when one person says, 'you don't get as much language choice as you think' and the other person says, 'you are clueless, because you're 4-6x more productive!' What a waste of time.
The World Is Complicated. Get Used To It.
Design is all about making hard choices and, hopefully, sometimes, hitting upon elegant solutions that solve conflicting goals.

When you can't solve conflicting goals, you have to be smart enough to decide which goal to solve, and not just be a lazy punter and pretend that adding a checkbox to the options dialog will solve it.
You have to make it light so it can be emptied easily. You have to make it heavy so it won't blow away.
key_insight: Mature thinking about technology and design requires holding contradictory truths simultaneously rather than collapsing complex tradeoffs into simplistic for-or-against positions.
